questions.question: State the laws of reflection of light. Draw a labelled ray diagram to show the reflection of light by a plane mirror and explain the characteristics of the image formed.
questions.answer: Laws of reflection: (i) The incident ray, the reflected ray, and the normal at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane. (ii) Angle of incidence equals angle of reflection (i = r). Image in plane mirror is virtual, erect, same size as object, laterally inverted, and as far behind the mirror as the object is in front.
answer_outline: Labelled ray diagram with i and r

questions.question: Define the terms: principal focus, focal length, centre of curvature, and radius of curvature for a spherical mirror. Draw ray diagrams to show image formation by a concave mirror when the object is placed (i) at infinity, (ii) beyond centre of curvature, (iii) at centre of curvature.
questions.answer: Principal focus: Point where parallel rays converge after reflection. Focal length: Distance from pole to focus. Centre of curvature: Centre of sphere. Radius of curvature: Twice focal length. Diagrams show: (i) image at focus (real, inverted, highly diminished), (ii) image between C and F (real, inverted, diminished), (iii) image at C (real, inverted, same size).
answer_outline: Three ray diagrams with object positions

questions.question: Derive the mirror formula for a spherical mirror. State the sign conventions used. Using the formula, find the focal length of a concave mirror if object distance is 30 cm and image distance is 15 cm (real image).
questions.answer: Mirror formula: 1/f = 1/v + 1/u. Sign convention (Cartesian): Object distance u negative, real image v negative, focal length concave negative. Derivation using similar triangles from ray diagram. Given u = -30 cm, v = -15 cm, 1/f = -1/15 - 1/30 = -1/10, f = -10 cm.

questions.question: Explain magnification produced by spherical mirrors. Derive the formula for linear magnification. An object 4 cm high is placed 20 cm in front of a concave mirror of focal length 15 cm. Find position, size, and nature of image.
questions.answer: Magnification m = h'/h = -v/u. Derivation from similar triangles. Using mirror formula: u = -20 cm, f = -15 cm, v = -60 cm, m = -3, image 12 cm high, real, inverted, enlarged, beyond C.
answer_outline: Sign of m for real/virtual images

questions.question: Why is a convex mirror preferred as a rear-view mirror in vehicles? Draw a ray diagram to justify your answer. Also, state two other uses of convex mirrors.
questions.answer: Convex mirror gives erect, virtual, diminished image and wider field of view, allowing driver to see more traffic. Ray diagram shows diverging rays forming virtual image behind mirror. Other uses: security mirrors in shops, street lights reflectors.
answer_outline: Reasons with field of view explanation

questions.question: State Snell's law of refraction. Define refractive index. Explain with a diagram why a pencil partially immersed in water appears bent.
questions.answer: Snell's law: sin i / sin r = constant (refractive index). Refractive index μ = speed in vacuum / speed in medium. Diagram shows light bending towards normal entering water, apparent bend due to refraction making bottom part appear raised.
answer_outline: Diagram and explanation of apparent bend

questions.question: Derive the expression for apparent depth when an object is placed in a denser medium like water. Why do stars twinkle?
questions.answer: Apparent depth d' = real depth d / μ. Derivation using small angle approximation and Snell's law. Twinkling due to atmospheric refraction; starlight bends irregularly through varying density layers, changing apparent position.

questions.question: Explain total internal reflection with conditions. Draw a labelled diagram to show total internal reflection in an optical fibre and mention one use.
questions.answer: TIR occurs when light goes from denser to rarer medium and i > critical angle. Critical angle C = sin⁻¹(1/μ). Diagram shows light ray in fibre bouncing internally due to TIR. Use: transmitting signals in optical fibre communication.

questions.question: Draw ray diagrams to show image formation by a convex lens when object is placed (i) beyond 2F, (ii) at 2F, (iii) between F and 2F. State nature and position of image in each case.
questions.answer: Diagrams: (i) Beyond 2F → image between F and 2F, real, inverted, diminished. (ii) At 2F → image at 2F, real, inverted, same size. (iii) Between F and 2F → image beyond 2F, real, inverted, enlarged.

questions.question: Derive the lens formula for a thin lens. State the sign convention. Calculate the focal length if object distance is 25 cm and image distance is 75 cm for a convex lens (real image).
questions.answer: Lens formula: 1/f = 1/v - 1/u. Sign convention: object u negative, real image v positive, convex f positive. Given u = -25 cm, v = +75 cm, 1/f = 1/75 + 1/25 = 4/75, f = 18.75 cm.

questions.question: Define power of a lens. Write its SI unit. A convex lens forms a real image three times larger than object at 30 cm from lens. Find focal length and power of lens.
questions.answer: Power P = 1/f (in metres), unit dioptre (D). Given m = -3 (real inverted), v = 30 cm, u = v/m = -10 cm. 1/f = 1/30 - 1/(-10) = 4/30, f = 7.5 cm = 0.075 m, P = 13.33 D.

questions.question: Explain why convex lens is used as a magnifying glass. Draw ray diagram for virtual enlarged image formation. State two other uses of convex lens.
questions.answer: Object between F and lens forms virtual, erect, enlarged image on same side. Diagram shows rays diverging after lens, appearing from enlarged virtual image. Other uses: microscope objective, projector lens.

questions.question: A concave lens always forms virtual, erect, and diminished image. Justify with ray diagram. An object 5 cm high is placed 20 cm from concave lens of focal length 10 cm. Find image position, size, and nature.
questions.answer: Diagram shows diverging rays forming virtual image on same side, diminished and erect. Using lens formula: u = -20 cm, f = -10 cm, v = -6.67 cm, m = +0.333, image 1.67 cm high, virtual, erect, diminished.

questions.question: Explain dispersion of white light through a glass prism with a labelled diagram. Why do different colours deviate differently?
questions.answer: Diagram shows white light splitting into spectrum (VIBGYOR) due to different wavelengths refracting at different angles. Violet deviates most (shortest wavelength, higher μ), red least. Dispersion occurs because refractive index varies with wavelength.

questions.question: What is atmospheric refraction? Explain with diagram why sun appears flattened at sunrise/sunset and advanced sunrise/delayed sunset.
questions.answer: Atmospheric refraction: bending of starlight through varying density layers. Diagram shows sun below horizon appearing above due to refraction. Flattening due to differential refraction (lower limb more refracted). Sunrise earlier, sunset later by ~2 minutes.
answer_outline: Explanation of flattening and time shift

questions.question: Describe an experiment to determine focal length of convex lens using distant object method. Mention precautions.
questions.answer: Place lens facing distant object (window/sun), adjust screen for sharp image, measure lens to screen distance = f. Repeat for average. Precautions: object very distant, lens holder perpendicular, no parallax error.

questions.question: An object is placed at 40 cm from a concave mirror of focal length 20 cm. Find image position, magnification, and nature using mirror formula. Draw ray diagram.
questions.answer: u = -40 cm, f = -20 cm, 1/v = 1/f - 1/u = -1/20 + 1/40 = -1/40, v = -40 cm. m = -v/u = -1, image real, inverted, same size at centre of curvature.

questions.question: Explain the working of a simple microscope using convex lens. Draw ray diagram for magnified virtual image. State magnification formula.
questions.answer: Object between F and lens forms enlarged virtual image. Diagram shows rays diverging, eye sees enlarged image. Magnification m = 1 + D/f (normal adjustment), or D/f + 1 (least distance).

questions.question: Why does light bend when passing from one medium to another? Explain with Snell's law and diagram for refraction from air to glass.
questions.answer: Light changes speed in different media, causing bending (refraction). Snell's law: μ = sin i / sin r. Diagram shows ray bending towards normal entering glass (denser medium, slower speed).

questions.question: A convex lens of focal length 20 cm forms a real image at 60 cm from lens. Find object distance and magnification. If object height is 3 cm, find image height.
questions.answer: f = +20 cm, v = +60 cm, 1/u = 1/f - 1/v = 1/20 - 1/60 = 1/30, u = -30 cm. m = -v/u = -2, image height = -6 cm (inverted, enlarged).

questions.question: Explain the formation of rainbow with diagram. Why is it observed in the opposite direction to the sun?
questions.answer: Rainbow forms by refraction, dispersion, TIR, and refraction in raindrops. Diagram shows white light splitting, TIR inside drop, violet inside red outside. Opposite to sun because observer sees reflected light from drops at 42° (red) to 40° (violet).

questions.question: Describe an experiment to trace the path of ray through glass slab and find refractive index. State observations and conclusion.
questions.answer: Fix slab, trace incident ray with pins, emergent ray aligned, measure i, r, lateral shift. μ = sin i / sin r (average). Observation: emergent parallel to incident but shifted. Conclusion: refraction bends ray, slab causes lateral displacement.

questions.question: Compare image formation by concave and convex mirrors for different object positions in a table. Mention one use each.
questions.answer: Table: object position, image position, nature, size for both mirrors. Concave use: shaving mirror (enlarged erect). Convex use: rear-view mirror (wide field, erect diminished).

questions.question: Explain the term 'power of accommodation' of eye lens. How does it relate to near point and far point? Why do we need corrective lenses for defects?
questions.answer: Power of accommodation: ability of eye lens to change focal length for near/far objects. Near point ~25 cm, far point infinity in normal eye. Defects like myopia (concave lens) or hypermetropia (convex lens) correct far/near point issues.

questions.question: Discuss the role of mirrors and lenses in optical instruments like simple microscope or telescope. Draw a labelled ray diagram for simple microscope and explain magnification.
questions.answer: Convex lens as objective/eyepiece forms enlarged images. Diagram for simple microscope: object between F and lens, virtual enlarged image. Magnification = 1 + D/f (angular). Helps see tiny objects clearly.
